fields.name: Attention-Deficit/Hyperactivity Disorder (ADHD)
fields.definition: A developmental disorder marked by persistent patterns of inattention, hyperactivity, and impulsivity that interfere with functioning or development. Symptoms begin in childhood and can affect social relationships, school performance, and work. Some people have mostly inattention symptoms; others mostly hyperactivity-impulsivity; and some have both.
fields.example: An adult with ADHD may struggle to sustain attention during meetings, frequently lose important items, and feel restless even when physically still.

fields.name: Inattentive Presentation (ADHD)
fields.definition: A presentation of ADHD in which a person shows six or more symptoms of inattention (five or more for adults over 16) but does not meet criteria for hyperactivity-impulsivity. Symptoms include difficulty sustaining attention, being easily distracted, failing to follow through on tasks, losing things, and forgetfulness. Girls and women are more commonly diagnosed with this presentation.
fields.example: Frequently missing details in work documents, leaving tasks unfinished, and appearing to not listen when spoken to directly.

fields.name: Hyperactive-Impulsive Presentation (ADHD)
fields.definition: A presentation of ADHD in which a person shows six or more symptoms of hyperactivity-impulsivity (five or more for adults over 16) without meeting criteria for inattention. Symptoms include fidgeting, leaving seats when expected to remain seated, talking excessively, interrupting others, and difficulty waiting for one's turn. Boys and men are more commonly diagnosed with this presentation.
fields.example: Standing up during meetings, blurting out answers before questions are finished, and having difficulty waiting in line.

fields.name: Combined Presentation (ADHD)
fields.definition: The most common ADHD presentation, in which a person meets criteria for both inattentive and hyperactive-impulsive presentations. The person shows six or more symptoms from each symptom cluster (five or more for adults over 16). This presentation often results in the broadest impact on daily functioning.
fields.example: A student who both daydreams during lectures and cannot stay seated, turns in incomplete work, and speaks out of turn in class.

fields.name: Executive Function
fields.definition: A set of mental processes that help people plan, focus, remember instructions, and manage multiple tasks. Executive functions include working memory, cognitive flexibility, and inhibitory control. ADHD significantly impairs executive function, making it difficult to initiate tasks, organize, manage time, regulate emotions, and follow through on intentions — even when the person understands what needs to be done.
fields.example: Knowing a deadline is tomorrow but being unable to start the task, or frequently forgetting commitments despite meaning to keep them.

fields.body: A provider collects information from multiple sources — the person, family members, teachers, or partners — and may review past school records or reports. The provider looks for at least six symptoms of inattention and/or hyperactivity-impulsivity (five for adults over 16) that have been present for at least six months, appear in two or more settings (e.g., home and work), began before age 12, and cause meaningful impairment. Psychological tests may assess working memory, executive function, and cognitive ability. Other conditions — sleep disorders, anxiety, depression — that mimic ADHD are ruled out before a diagnosis is confirmed.
fields.name: ADHD Diagnosis (DSM-5 Process)
fields.example: A clinician interviews an adult patient and their spouse, reviews childhood report cards, and administers a standardized behavior rating scale before diagnosing Combined Presentation ADHD.

fields.name: Stimulant Medications for ADHD
fields.definition: The most commonly prescribed medications for ADHD. They work by increasing levels of dopamine and norepinephrine in the brain — neurotransmitters that play key roles in attention, thinking, and motivation. Examples include methylphenidate (e.g., Ritalin, Concerta) and amphetamine-based medications (e.g., Adderall, Vyvanse). Up to 70–80% of people with ADHD respond to stimulant treatment.
fields.example: Methylphenidate taken in the morning reduces distractibility and improves task completion for a student throughout the school day.

fields.body: Stimulant medications block the reuptake of dopamine and norepinephrine into the presynaptic neuron, increasing the availability of these neurotransmitters in the synapse. Elevated dopamine improves signaling in the prefrontal cortex — the brain region responsible for executive function, attention, and impulse control. This effect reverses the prefrontal cortex-dependent cognitive deficits central to ADHD. Stimulants work rapidly, often within an hour. They are the primary pharmacologic treatment for ADHD and are most effective as long-acting formulations.
fields.name: How Stimulant Medication Works (ADHD)
fields.example: An amphetamine medication blocks dopamine reuptake, raising dopamine levels in the prefrontal cortex, which improves sustained attention and reduces impulsivity.

fields.name: Behavioral Therapy for ADHD
fields.definition: A non-medication treatment approach that addresses the core behavioral and cognitive features of ADHD. For children, it often involves parent training to manage and reinforce desired behaviors. For adults, cognitive behavioral therapy (CBT) helps challenge unhelpful thought patterns and build practical skills — organization, time management, planning, and emotional regulation. CBT addresses the psychological layer of ADHD that medication alone does not fully treat.
fields.example: An adult with ADHD works with a therapist to build a daily planning routine and use external reminders, reducing missed appointments.

fields.question: What percentage of children in the US have been diagnosed with ADHD?
fields.answer: Approximately 11% of children had been diagnosed with ADHD based on parent-report survey data (National Survey of Children's Health). This represented a 42% increase from 7.8% in 2003 to 11.0% in 2011. Among adolescents aged 13–18, lifetime prevalence was 8.7% based on diagnostic interview data.

fields.question: Before what age must ADHD symptoms appear for a diagnosis to be made?
fields.answer: Symptoms must have been present before age 12. Diagnosing ADHD in adulthood is complicated by the need to demonstrate that symptoms began before this age, often requiring review of childhood records or reports from family members.

fields.question: How many ADHD symptoms must adults (over age 16) show for a diagnosis?
fields.answer: Adults and youth over 16 must show at least 5 symptoms of inattention, hyperactivity-impulsivity, or both — compared to the 6-symptom threshold required for children up to age 16.

fields.question: Does ADHD persist into adulthood?
fields.answer: For many people it does, though symptoms often change in presentation. It is estimated that approximately 4% of adults live with ADHD, compared with 11% in those younger than 18. Research suggests approximately one-third of children diagnosed with ADHD retain a full diagnosis in adulthood, though many others continue to experience significant symptoms that affect functioning.

fields.title: ADHD and Comorbidities
fields.body: ADHD rarely travels alone. Adults with ADHD are significantly more likely than people without ADHD to also have an anxiety disorder, major depressive disorder, or a substance use disorder. Research shows odds ratios of approximately 5.0 for anxiety disorders, 4.5 for major depressive disorder, and 4.6 for substance use disorders compared to adults without ADHD. These co-occurring conditions can make ADHD harder to identify and treat. Combined medication and psychosocial treatment is generally considered the most beneficial approach when comorbidities are present.
